content.problemStatement:
Compute:

 floor[1*e] + floor[2*e] + floor[3*e] + ... + floor[**n***e],

where floor[x] is the largest integer that is not greater than x, and e is Euler's number: 2.7182818284...
content.inputFormat: A single line containing one integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^7).
content.outputFormat: A single line containing the integer sum_{i=1}^{n} floor(i·e).

solution.approach:
The value floor(i·e) can be obtained directly using the built‑in floating point constant e. For each i from 1 to n we compute floor(i*e) and accumulate the result. Because e is irrational the floating point approximation is sufficient for n up to 10^7 – the error of double precision is far smaller than 1, so the floor operation is always correct. The algorithm runs in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
If the language supports arbitrary‑precision arithmetic (e.g., Python's Decimal), the same idea works for larger n, but the simple double version satisfies the given limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few scalar variables.
